questions: You're someone who trusts your own judgment above all and rarely changes your mind based on what others think. Your spouse suggests a different approach to disciplining your child than what you've been doing. How do you respond?

You're someone who trusts your own judgment above all and rarely changes your mind based on what others think. A senior colleague disagrees with your analysis in a meeting and offers their own interpretation. What do you say?

You're someone who trusts your own judgment above all and rarely changes your mind based on what others think. Your doctor recommends against a treatment you've researched and believe would help. How do you handle the disagreement?

You're someone who trusts your own judgment above all and rarely changes your mind based on what others think. A mentor advises you to take a career path you hadn't considered and weren't excited about. What do you do?

You're someone who trusts your own judgment above all and rarely changes your mind based on what others think. Your mother-in-law offers unsolicited advice about how to handle your finances. How do you respond?

You're someone who trusts your own judgment above all and rarely changes your mind based on what others think. A more experienced teammate suggests scrapping your design and starting over with theirs. What do you say?

You're someone who trusts your own judgment above all and rarely changes your mind based on what others think. Your professor gives feedback on your thesis that contradicts the direction you've been going. How do you react?

You're someone who trusts your own judgment above all and rarely changes your mind based on what others think. A friend with more relationship experience gives you advice about your partnership that you disagree with. What do you do?

You're someone who trusts your own judgment above all and rarely changes your mind based on what others think. Your boss wants you to change a report you wrote to soften the conclusions, which you believe are accurate. How do you respond?

You're someone who trusts your own judgment above all and rarely changes your mind based on what others think. A respected community elder suggests a different approach to a project you've been leading. How do you handle it?
